IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and under-specified problem—evaluating creative NLG—by proposing a comprehensive manual evaluation for fluency/coherence/style-matching and an improved fully automated rhyme-density-based method that removes manual intervention (sections 4–6). It also releases a dataset of lyrics for 13 rap artists annotated for style, enabling reproducible comparisons and follow-up work. These contributions are likely to substantially influence research on creative text generation and evaluation methods, and to be adopted within the lyric/poetry NLG subcommunity, though they are not fully transformative across all NLP.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper assembles and formalizes an evaluation suite for rap-lyric ghostwriting (manual annotations for fluency/coherence/style-matching and automated rhyme-density/similarity regressions) and fully automates a previously semi-automatic method (Potash et al. 2015) by adding a repetition penalty and providing an annotated 13-artist corpus. These components largely reuse established metrics and prior work (Hirjee & Brown’s rhyme-density, Potash et al.’s methodology, standard LSTM/ngram baselines) and primarily integrate/extend them rather than introduce a fundamentally new algorithm or theoretical perspective, so the novelty is moderate and incremental.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a reasonably broad set of experiments across 11–13 artists using both manual (line-level fluency/coherence, style-matching in Sec. 6.2) and automated metrics (rhyme density and similarity regressions in Sec. 6.3), and compares an LSTM to n-gram baselines. However, there are no explicit ablation studies on model components or sensitivity analyses beyond iteration / n‑gram sweeps, and many reproduction details (model architecture, hyperparameters, training schedule) are left unspecified or deferred to Potash et al., limiting reproducibility. The authors do note limitations (e.g., LSTM inability to integrate new vocabulary and negative iteration artifacts), so some failure modes are discussed, but deeper analysis of failure cases and more thorough experimental probing are missing.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a roughly logical structure (dataset → evaluation methodology → experiments → conclusion) but presentation is marred by extensive repetition and copy-paste artifacts (e.g., the paragraph beginning “One example is ’Deep Art’...” and several paragraphs about Belz and Kow appear verbatim multiple times). Important items are inconsistently formatted or missing (tables 1–3 are referenced but not present in the excerpt; placeholders like “Left blank for review” and raw footnote URLs remain). Notation and formulas are awkward or unclear (e.g., “Match% = #m#a” lacks a clear operator), and there are numerous grammatical/typo issues and stray LaTeX/meta lines that reduce readability and require substantial editing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its LSTM model against an n-gram baseline and directly builds on/automates the semi-automatic evaluation of Potash et al. (2015) (see Sections 4–6 and repeated mentions that they replicate Potash et al.’s Fabolous results), and it uses established metrics such as Hirjee and Brown’s rhyme density for fair metric-based comparison. However, the work omits other contemporary neural or lyric-specific generative baselines beyond the n-gram and Potash’s RNN, and some experimental parity details (e.g., exact training/data-split equivalence and baseline tuning) are not fully specified, so comparisons are good but have minor omissions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a plausible evaluation pipeline (dataset collected from OHHLA with heuristic preprocessing and filtering of verses <20 tokens; manual annotation protocol with 5 verses/artist and #a=40; automated metrics using rhyme density and maximum-similarity regression from Potash et al.; LSTM trained per-artist for ≥16,400 iterations, verses sampled every 2,000). However, in the provided text the core supporting evidence is missing (Tables 1–3 and numeric results are not shown), there are no reported statistical tests of significance, and key methodological details are underspecified (how maximum similarity is computed, how repetition is automatically penalized, and handling of noisy/non-verse lines). These omissions create logical gaps between the claims (e.g., that the automated method replicates Potash et al. and that the LSTM generalizes better than the baseline across 11 artists) and the evidence shown here, so the work is technically promising but incompletely substantiated in the excerpt.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper proposes a comprehensive evaluation methodology for creative NLG (ghostwriting rap lyrics), releases an annotated corpus of 13 artists, and combines manual (fluency/coherence/style-matching) and automated (rhyme-density, similarity/regression) evaluation—components that are squarely within top-tier NLP interests in NLG and evaluation. It compares against baselines (n‑gram) and an LSTM generative model and situates itself relative to prior work (Potash et al., Hirjee & Brown, Malmi et al.), matching the audience, problem formulation, and evaluation standards expected at major NLP venues.
